title: 🎙️ Indigenous Lands & Innovation
description: Today we explore how Cariboo and BC Indigenous nations are using technology on their own terms — for language revitalization, land stewardship, cultural preservation, and self-determined governance. We're looking for stories that centre Indigenous voices and leadership in innovation, where technology serves community sovereignty rather than replacing it. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we explore how Cariboo and BC Indigenous nations are using technology on their own terms — for language revitalization, land stewardship, cultural preservation, and self-determined governance. We're looking for stories that centre Indigenous voices and leadership in innovation, where technology serves community sovereignty rather than replacing it.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Indigenous Lands & Innovation' — Indigenous nations using technology, governance, and stewardship on their own terms.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder. Indigenous-led journalism on governance, rights, and self-determination should score in the moderate-to-good range even without an explicit technology angle — do not collapse everything that lacks a 'tech' hook to near 0:
- 70-100: Cariboo-specific stories bridging Indigenous governance/culture AND technology — Tŝilhqot'in/Secwépemc/Dakelh language revitalization tech, guardian programs using sensors/drones, GIS land mapping, Indigenous-led connectivity, digital cultural heritage projects.
- 45-69: Pan-Canadian Indigenous governance, sovereignty, land rights, treaty, or reconciliation journalism — APTN, Eagle Feather News, or IndigiNews-style coverage of First Nations governance, MMIWG, land claims, self-determination, or Indigenous-led services — even without a technology angle, per the curator's standing interest in Indigenous governance and sovereignty.
- 25-44: Indigenous cultural, community, or human-interest stories (awards, events, ceremonies) with limited governance or tech substance, or BC land-stewardship/environmental stories with meaningful First Nations involvement.
- 10-24: General BC land, environmental, or forestry stories that mention Indigenous communities only in passing.
- 0-9: No Indigenous angle at all.

Strong fits include: language revitalization technology for Tŝilhqot'in/Secwépemc/Dakelh, Indigenous guardian programs using remote monitoring and sensors, land management tech and environmental monitoring on Indigenous lands, digital tools for traditional knowledge preservation, community mapping and GIS for land claims, Indigenous-led connectivity projects, technology for cultural heritage documentation, mobile apps for language learning, drone/satellite monitoring for stewardship, reconciliation through digital collaboration, how Cariboo Indigenous nations use innovation for self-determination, and Indigenous governance, sovereignty, treaty, and land-rights journalism from any Canadian nation. Prefer articles that bridge Indigenous governance/culture AND technology, but governance/sovereignty journalism alone is a solid fit. Penalise heavily (score 0–9): international geopolitical and war news with no Indigenous rights angle; general wildfire and BC Wildlife Service updates not led by or directly impacting Indigenous communities or guardians; provincial forestry and environmental policy without explicit First Nations involvement; consumer tech and gadget reviews; science discoveries without Indigenous stewardship or sovereignty context; crime and judicial news unrelated to Indigenous rights. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] Outrage as woman jailed for three years after criticising Somali government online
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

Sentencing of 27-year-old Sadia Moalim Ali condemned by former president and prime ministers as well as rights groups

A rickshaw driver in Somalia has been sentenced to three years in prison for comments she made on social media, in a case that has caught the public’s attention and provoked outrage in the country.

Sadia Moalim Ali, a 27-year-old nursing graduate, was originally charged with insulting government institutions and incitement to commit a crime, but convicted only of the former. Her sentence, immediately condemned as “fundamentally unjust”, was handed down on 25 June.

Continue reading...

items.title: [Boing Boing] Americans now lose a quarter of a trillion dollars a year gambling
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

Americans will lose about $250 billion gambling this year, reports Joey Politano, a number that's up 60% in half a decade. And that doesn't include "prediction markets" and cryptocurrencies, both mostly vehicles for more gambling. Politicians are well-greased by the industry lobbyists, personal debt is soaring out of sight, and there's no immediate prospect of anyone taking control. — Read the rest

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Convent of Santa Maria de Mave in Santa María de Mave, Spain
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

 This building is in the village of Santa Maria  de Mave ,  The village takes  name from the church and convento at its centre and is close to the larger settlement of Mave close to the border of  Cantabria and Castile y Leòn .

In Spain the term  Convento is used to distinguish the institution  as housing a group of religious members of an order which is active in the community  in which it is based as opposed to a contemplative, isolated  order in which case the building would be termed a monestery. To a certain  extent the terms are interchangeable  and the term "convent" certainly  does not imply that the residents were nuns. In this case the occupants were male friars. The term Monastario is often applied nowadays to the church building directly attached to the Convento with the Romanesque church being in its original religious form (with with a triple apse and three naves,  a bell wall, currently with resident white storks, and a tall dome over the transept). The Convento has been converted from the cloister (rebuilt in the 18th century) to a boutique hotel with a restaurant  and a bar/cafeteria. The conversion  has been done sympathetically with many original feature retained but the architect clearly has a love for the modern. Some of the bedrooms are actually in the monk's cells.

The origins of the Convento and the associated church date as far back as the second half of the 9th century. The abbey was founded by King Alphonse III . In 1033, Mave and its possessions became part of the important Benedictine monastery of San Salvador de Oña, in Burgos. Nothing remains of the ancient Pre-Romanesque structure.
items.summary: This building is in the village of Santa Maria de Mave , The village takes name from the church and convento at its centre and is close to the larger settlement of Mave close to the border of Cantabria and Castile y Leòn . In Spain the term Convento is used to distinguish the institution as housing

items.title: [New Atlas] Google looks to electronic waste as cost-effective AI server solution
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

Every year, billions of phones are discarded globally, many of them with perfectly usable processors. At the same time, the tech industry is preparing to spend billions on new AI computing hardware, at high environmental costs. Google, in collaboration with researchers at the University of California, San Diego, is developing a way to bridge those two realities by building a server from recovered phone processors, tackling a waste problem while providing low-carbon computing.

Continue Reading

Category: Computing, Consumer Tech, Technology

Tags: Processing, Mobile Phones, Cell+Phone, Chips
